ai_strategy.summary: University of California, San Francisco's public AI posture combines published guidance, teaching resources, research centers, AI-relevant programs, and research computing. Public evidence points to distributed governance rather than a verified standalone chief AI officer.

key_provisions: Use appropriate or approved AI tools within institutional data rules
Faculty should set clear course-level AI expectations
Users remain responsible for accuracy, attribution, privacy, and academic integrity
Sensitive data should not be entered into unapproved public tools
ai_policy.summary: UCSF publishes AI service guidance and teaching guidance in a health-sciences context where privacy, clinical risk, and data governance are central.

bodies.name: AI technology and teaching guidance owners
bodies.scope: Distributed responsibility for AI tool guidance, teaching support, and research coordination.

bodies.name: UCSF AI and Bakar Computational Health Sciences Institute

governance_structure.summary: University of California, San Francisco's AI governance is distributed across IT, teaching support, and research units.

initiatives.title: University of California, San Francisco AI guidance and acceptable-use resources
initiatives.description: UCSF publishes AI service guidance and teaching guidance in a health-sciences context where privacy, clinical risk, and data governance are central.
initiatives.significance: Institution-level guidance is the baseline signal for responsible AI adoption.

investment.description: Policy and guidance work; no dollar amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: AI use is permitted within privacy, security, course, and attribution guardrails.

initiatives.title: University of California, San Francisco generative AI teaching resources
initiatives.description: University of California, San Francisco publishes faculty-facing resources for syllabus language, assignments, assessment, and student expectations around generative AI.
initiatives.significance: Teaching guidance moves AI adoption from conduct enforcement into course design.

investment.description: Faculty support resources; no amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Faculty set course-level rules and students need clear disclosure and permitted-use expectations.

initiatives.title: UCSF AI and Bakar Computational Health Sciences Institute
initiatives.description: University of California, San Francisco has AI, machine learning, data science, policy, health, or autonomy research structures that organize faculty and graduate research.
initiatives.significance: Dedicated research structures are a core marker of R1 AI maturity.

investment.description: Research center/institute support; aggregate AI-specific amount not disclosed.

collaborations.description: Cross-school or interdisciplinary AI research ecosystem.

initiatives.title: Generative AI teaching resources for health sciences
initiatives.description: University of California, San Francisco offers AI-relevant degrees, certificates, bootcamps, or structured learning pathways in AI, machine learning, data science, analytics, or computing.
initiatives.significance: AI credentials connect R1 research capacity to student and employer demand.

initiatives.title: University of California, San Francisco research computing for AI workloads
initiatives.description: UCSF Wynton HPC supports computational biomedical research, including AI and machine-learning workloads.
initiatives.significance: AI maturity depends on scalable compute as well as policy and curricula.

investment.description: Research computing infrastructure; AI-specific public amount not disclosed.

initiatives.title: University of California, San Francisco responsible and equitable AI access
initiatives.description: University of California, San Francisco's public AI resources stress transparent expectations, responsible use, and access-aware teaching or public-interest AI work.
initiatives.significance: Equity evidence distinguishes mature AI adoption from simple tool availability.

investment.description: Access and responsible-use work; no amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Clear AI expectations reduce unequal access, ambiguous enforcement, and privacy risk.

initiatives.title: UCSF ChatGPT Enterprise migration from Versa
initiatives.description: UCSF announced ChatGPT Enterprise for early 2026, replacing Versa Chat and migrating about 9,000 current Versa Chat users plus a later phase for 100+ Versa Assistants.
initiatives.significance: This closes platform usage with one of the strongest public user-count disclosures in the sweep.

investment.description: No public contract value disclosed for ChatGPT Enterprise.

collaborations.description: OpenAI ChatGPT Enterprise will replace Versa Chat for general-purpose chatbot use.

initiatives.policy_implications: Enterprise safeguards include privacy and HIPAA support while migrating existing Versa users.

initiatives.title: Bridges Curriculum generative AI usage policy
initiatives.description: UCSF Medical Education’s Bridges policy allows Versa for approved student, faculty, and staff use, prohibits AI in assessments unless faculty grant permission, and names the Associate Dean for Assessment as accountable dean/director.
initiatives.significance: This adds assessment-specific policy evidence and patient-data guardrails.

collaborations.description: Policy developed through UCSF curriculum and governance channels.

tools_and_vendors.name: Versa
tools_and_vendors.role: approved HIPAA-compliant AI tool for UCSF data in specific contexts

initiatives.policy_implications: Explicitly restricts AI use in evaluated work unless faculty permit it.

initiatives.title: Wynton HPC GPU capacity for AI research
initiatives.description: UCSF Wynton documentation reports 61 GPU nodes and 235 GPUs available across communal and contributed GPU resources.
initiatives.significance: This closes compute capacity with a public GPU count.

investment.description: Wynton is a co-op cluster supported by UCSF research faculty and institutional funds; no total public cost disclosed.

regulatory_context.summary: University of California, San Francisco operates under federal education, privacy, and research obligations plus University of California system rules. This sweep found institutional AI guidance but no separate campus-specific AI statute.

narrative_summary: University of California, San Francisco is an R1 institution whose public AI posture is visible in responsible-use guidance, classroom resources, AI-relevant research structures, credentials or workforce programming, and research computing. The campus permits AI experimentation with guardrails rather than presenting a simple prohibition. Faculty-facing materials make course policy and assessment design central to implementation, while technology guidance emphasizes privacy, security, attribution, and responsible use. Research evidence comes from AI, machine-learning, data-science, policy, health, autonomy, or computing organizations, supported by high-performance or research computing resources. The profile is strongest on public policy resources, research centers, programs, and infrastructure, and weaker on exact budgets, enterprise license counts, adoption metrics, and named executive ownership. Overall, the institution appears to be in a growing-to-operational phase: AI work is real and distributed across functions, but public evidence does not yet show a single centralized AI operating model with disclosed outcomes.

gaps_acknowledged: Current president, provost, CIO, and chief AI officer were not independently verified in this worker sweep, so leadership names are left null.
Exact enterprise AI contract values, user counts, and adoption metrics were not publicly confirmed.
AI-specific total budget was not disclosed in the public sources used.
No institution-specific AI litigation or major controversy was confirmed.
UCSF now discloses ChatGPT Enterprise/Versa usage and Wynton GPU capacity, but public sources still do not disclose enterprise contract values or full cost of the ChatGPT Enterprise migration.
Worker 3 did not find public subgroup-level student outcomes for the Bridges generative AI policy.
